Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2024

## LEARNING INTERACTIVE REAL-WORLD SIMULATORS

**Sherry Yang,**[1] _[,]_[2] **Yilun Du**[3] **Kamyar Ghasemipour**[2] **Jonathan Tompson**[2] **Leslie Kaelbling**[3] **Dale Schuurmans**[2] _[,]_[4] **Pieter Abbeel**[1] 1UC Berkeley 2Google DeepMind 3MIT 4University of Alberta sherryy@ _{_ berkeley.edu, google.com _}_

## ABSTRACT

Generative models trained on internet data have revolutionized how text, image, and video content can be created. Perhaps the next milestone for generative models is to simulate realistic experience in response to actions taken by humans, robots, and other interactive agents. Applications of a real-world simulator range from controllable content creation in games and movies, to training embodied agents purely in simulation that can be directly deployed in the real world. We explore the possibility of learning a universal simulator (UniSim) of real-world interaction through generative modeling. We first make the important observation that natural datasets available for learning a real-world simulator are often rich along different dimensions (e.g., abundant objects in image data, densely sampled actions in robotics data, and diverse movements in navigation data). With careful orchestration of diverse datasets, each providing a different aspect of the overall experience, we can simulate the visual outcome of both high-level instructions such as “open the drawer” and low-level controls such as “move by ∆ _x,_ ∆ _y_ ” from otherwise static scenes and objects. We use the simulator to train both high-level vision-language policies and low-level reinforcement learning policies, each of which can be deployed in the real world in zero shot after training purely in simulation. We also show that other types of intelligence such as video captioning models can benefit from training with simulated experience, opening up even wider applications. Video demos can be found at https://universal-simulator.github.io.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Generative models trained on internet data can now produce highly realistic text (OpenAI, 2023),image (Ramesh et al., 2022), and video (Ho et al., 2022a). Perhaps the ultimate goal of generative models is to be able to simulate the visual effects of a wide variety of actions, from how cars are driven on a street to how furniture and meals are prepared. With a real-world simulator, humans can “interact” with diverse scenes and objects, robots can learn from simulated experience without risking physical damage, and a vast amount of “real-world” like data can be simulated to train other types of machine intelligence.

One roadblock to building this simulator lies in the datasets — different datasets cover different information that have to be brought together to simulate realistic experience. For instance, paired text-image data from the internet contains rich scenes and objects but little movement (Schuhmann et al., 2022; Zhai et al., 2022), video captioning and question answering data contain rich highlevel descriptions but little low-level movement detail (Xu et al., 2016; Krishna et al., 2017), human activity data contains rich human action but little mechanical motion (Miech et al., 2019; Grauman et al., 2022), and robotics data contains rich robot action but are limited in quantity (Dasari et al., 2019; Mandlekar et al., 2018). Since different datasets are curated by different industrial or research communities for different purposes, divergence in information is natural and hard to overcome, posing difficulties to a real-world simulator that seeks to capture all visual aspects of the world.

In this work, we propose to combine a wealth of data in a conditional video generation framework to instantiate a universal simulator (UniSim)[1] . Under a unified action-in-video-out interface, the simulator enables rich interaction through fine-grained motion control of otherwise static scenes and objects. To support long-horizon repeated interactions, we formulate the simulator as an _observation_

While the potential applications of the simulator are broad, we demonstrate three specific use cases. We first show how the simulator enables a vision-language policy to perform long-horizon goal-conditioned tasks through hindsight relabeling of simulated experience (Andrychowicz et al., 2017). In addition to learning high-level vision-language policies, we illustrate how the simulator can enable learning low-level control policies by leveraging model-based reinforcement learning (RL) (Sutton, 1988). Both the high-level vision-language policy and the low-level control policy, while trained purely in simulation, can generalize to real robot settings. This is enabled by using the simulator that is nearly visually indistinguishable from the real world, achieving one step towards bridging the sim-to-real gap in embodied learning (Rusu et al., 2017). Furthermore, we can simulate rare events where data collection is expensive or dangerous (e.g., crashes in self-driving cars). Such simulated videos can then be used to improve other machine intelligence such as rare event detectors, suggesting broad applications of UniSim beyond embodied learning. The main contributions can be summarized as follows:

- We take the first step toward building a universal simulator of real-world interaction by combining diverse datasets rich in along different dimensions — e.g., objects, scenes, actions, motions, language, and motor controls — in a unified action-in-video-out generative framework.

- We formulate the action-in-video-out framework as an _observation prediction model_ conditioned on finite history and parametrized by a video diffusion model. We illustrate that the observation prediction model can be rolled out autoregressively to obtain consistent and long-horizon videos.

- We illustrate how the simulator can enable both high-level language policies, low-level control policies, and video captioning models to generalize to the real world when trained purely in simulation, thereby bridging the sim-to-real gap.

## 2 LEARNING AN INTERACTIVE REAL-WORLD SIMULATOR

We define a simulator of the real world as a model that, given some state of the world (e.g., an image frame), can take in some action as input, and produce the visual consequence of the action (in the form of a video) as output. Learning such a simulator is hard, since different actions have different formats (e.g.,language instructions, robot controls, camera movements) and videos have different frame rates. Nevertheless, we propose specific strategies for processing each type of data to unify the action space and align videos of variable lengths to actions in Section 2.1. With a unified action space, we then train an action-conditioned video generation model to fuse information across datasets through a universal interface relating actions to videos in Section 2.2.

## 2.1 ORCHESTRATING DIVERSE DATASETS

Below, we highlight diverse information in different datasets and propose ways to process actions into a common format (see all datasets used to train UniSim in Appendix B).

Figure 2: **Training and inference of UniSim.** UniSim is a video diffusion model trained to predict the next (variable length) set of observation frames ( _ot_ ) given observations from the past (e.g., _ot−_ 1) and action input _at−_ 1. UniSim can handle temporally extended actions in various modalities such as motor controls (∆ _x_ 1 _,_ ∆ _ω_ 1 _,_ ∆ _x_ 2 _, ..._ ), language descriptions (“wipe table”), and actions extracted from camera motions and other sources. Each dotted arrow indicates concatenating the initial noise sample for the next video segment with the previous frame.

- **Simulated execution and renderings.** While annotating actions for real-world videos is expensive, simulation engines such as Habitat (Savva et al., 2019) can render a wide variety of actions. We use datasets previously collected from these simulators, i.e., Habitat object navigation with HM3D (Ramakrishnan et al., 2021) and Language Table Data from Lynch et al. (2023) to train UniSim. We extract text descriptions as actions when available. For simulated continuous control actions, we encode them via language embeddings and concatenate the text embeddings with discretized control values.

- **Real robot data.** An increasing amount of video data of real-robot executions paired with task descriptions such as the Bridge Data (Ebert et al., 2021) and data that enabled RT-1 and RT-2 (Brohan et al., 2022) are becoming increasingly available. Despite low-level control actions often being different across robots, the task descriptions can serve as high-level actions in UniSim. We further include discretize continuous controls actions when available similar to simulated robotics data.

- **Human activity videos.** Rich human activity data such as Ego4D (Grauman et al., 2022), EPICKITCHENS (Damen et al., 2018), and Something-Something V2 (Goyal et al., 2017) have been curated. Different from low-level robot controls, these activities are high-level actions that humans take to interact with the world. But these actions are often provided as labels for video classification or activity recognition tasks (Goyal et al., 2017). In this case, we convert the video labels into text actions. In addition, we subsample the videos to construct chunks of observations at a frame rate that captures meaningful actions.

- **Panorama scans.** There exists a wealth of 3D scans such as Matterport3D (Chang et al., 2017). These static scans do not contain actions. We construct actions (e.g., turn left) by truncating panorama scans and utilize the information of camera poses between two images.

- **Internet text-image data.** Paired text-image datasets such as LAION (Schuhmann et al., 2021) contain static images of a variety of objects without actions. However, the captions often contain motion information such as “a person _walking_ ”. To use image data in UniSim, we treat individual images as single-frame videos and image captions as actions.

For each of these datasets, we process text tokens into continuous representations using T5 language model embeddings (Raffel et al., 2020) concatenated with low-level actions such as robot controls. This serves as the final unified action space of our simulator.

2.2 SIMULATING LONG-HORIZON INTERACTIONS THROUGH OBSERVATION PREDICTION

With observations from different environments that have been converted to videos, and actions of different formats that have been converted to continuous embeddings, we can formulate interactions with many real-world environments as interacting with a universal simulator. We then formulate the universal simulator as an _observation prediction model_ that predicts observations conditioned on actions and previous observations as shown in Figure 2. We finally show that this observation prediction model can be parametrized using video diffusion.

**Simulating Real-World Interactions.** We define an observation space _O_ and an action space _A_ which capture the videos and actions described in Section 2.1. At a specific interactive step _t_ , an agent, having observed a set of history frames _ht−_ 1 _∈ O_ , decides on some temporally extended action _at−_ 1 _∈ A_ , which can be resolved into a sequence of low-level robot commands to be executed in

3

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2024

the real world. During the execution, the next set of video frames _ot ∈ O_ are captured from the real world. The goal of a simulator is to predict _ot_ from _ht−_ 1 and _at−_ 1. We can formulate this prediction problem as learning an _observation prediction_ model _p_ ( _ot|ht−_ 1 _, at−_ 1). While an ideal predictive model should condition on all information of the past, i.e., ( _o_ 0 _, a_ 0 _. . . , at−_ 2 _, ot−_ 1), through some recurrent state, we found conditioning on a finite set of frames (e.g., frames from the most recent interaction, _ot−_ 1) greatly simplifies the modeling problem. To simulate long interactions, we can sample from the observation prediction model _p_ ( _ot|ht−_ 1 _, at−_ 1) autoregressively conditioned on the previously sampled observations. One advantage of this observation prediction model is that the simulator stays the same across all tasks and can be used in combination with any reward function, which can be separately learned. The learned reward function can then be used to optimize policies _π_ ( _at|ht_ ) using existing decision making algorithms such as planning and RL, as we will illustrate in Section 4.1 and Section 4.2.

**Parametrizing and Training the Simulator.** We parametrize _p_ ( _ot|ht−_ 1 _, at−_ 1) using diffusion models (Sohl-Dickstein et al., 2015; Ho et al., 2020) as an instantiation of UniSim outlined in Figure 2. Specifically, the reverse process learns a denoising model _ϵθ_ ( _o_[(] _t[k]_[)] _, k|ht−_ 1 _, at−_ 1) that, conditioned on the history, generates the next observationfrom initial noise samples using _K_ denoising steps. In practice, we only use previous video frames and omit previous actions as history, and concatenate previous video frames with initial noise samples _o_[(] _t[K]_[)] _∼N_ (0 _, I_ ) channelwise to serve as conditional inputs to the denoising model. **Architecture and Training.** We use the video U-Net architecture (Ho et al., 2022b) to implement UniSim by employing interleaved temporal and spatial attention and convolution layers in both the downsampling and upsampling passes. For history conditioning, we replicate the conditioning frames at all future frame indices, and concatenate the conditioning frames with the noise sample for each of the future frame to serve as input to the U-Net. UniSim model has 5.6B parameters and requires 512 TPU-v3 and 20 days to train on all data. See more details in Appendix C.

3 SIMULATING REAL-WORLD INTERACTIONS We now demonstrate emulating real-world manipulation and navigation environments by simulating both action-rich and long-horizon interactions for both humans and robots.

3.1 ACTION-RICH, LONG-HORIZON, AND DIVERSE INTERACTIONS

**Action-Rich Simulation.** We first demonstrate action-rich interactions through natural language actions. Figure 3 shows simulation of human manipulation and navigation starting from the same initial observation (left-most column). We can instruct a person in the initial frame to perform various kitchen tasks (top left), press different switches (top right), or navigate scenes (bottom). The model only trained on generic internet data, without action-rich manipulation data such as EPICKITCHENS (Damen et al., 2018), fails to simulate action-rich manipulations (Appendix F).

**Long-Horizon Simulation.** Next, we illustrate 8 _sequential_ interactions in Figure 4. We condition the simulation of each interaction on previous observations and new language action as described in Section 2.2. UniSim successfully preserves objects manipulated by previous instructions (e.g., the orange and can are preserved in the drawers in Columns 4, 5, 7, 8 after being put in the drawers). See additional long-horizon interactions in Appendix A.1.

**Diversity and Stochasticity in the Simulator.** UniSim can also support highly diverse and stochastic environment transitions, e.g., diverse objects being revealed after removing the towel on top (Figure 5 left), diverse object colors and locations (cups and pens in Figure 5 right), and real-world variabilities such as change in camera angles. Flexibility in diffusion models promotes simulation of highly stochastic environments that cannot be controlled by actions, so that a policy can learn to only control the controllable part (Yang et al., 2022).

Conditioning on 4 frames is better than conditioning on a single frame, but conditioning on history that is too far in the past (4 frames with exponentially increasing distances) can hurt performance. Increasing the number of conditioning frames beyond 4 did not further improve performance on Ego4D, but it could be helpful for applications that require memory from distant past (e.g., navigation for retrieval).

**Simulating Low-Data Domains.** During joint training of UniSim on diverse data, we found that na¨ıvely combining datasets of highly varying size can result in low generation quality in low-data domains. While we can increase the weight of these domains in the data mixture during training, we found that attaching a domain identifier such as the name of the dataset to the actions being conditioned on improves generation quality in low-data domains, as shown in Figure 6. While such domain identifier improves in-distribution generation quality, we found domain-specific identifiers to hurt generalization to other domains, and should only be applied with the test domain is in distribution of the training domain.

## 4 APPLICATIONS OF UNISIM

We now demonstrate how UniSim can be used to train other types of machine intelligence such as vision-language policies, RL agents, and vision-language models through simulating highly realistic experiences.

- 4.1 TRAINING LONG-HORIZON VISION-LANGUAGE POLICIES THROUGH HINDSIGHT LABELING.

Language models and vision language models (VLM) have recently been used as policies that can operate in image or text based observation and action spaces (Du et al., 2023b; Driess et al., 2023; Brohan et al., 2023). One major challenge in learning such agents lies in the need for large amounts of language action labels. The labor intensity in data collection only increases as tasks increase in horizon and complexity. UniSim can generate large amounts of training data for VLM policies through hindsight relabeling.

**Setup and Baseline.** We use data from the Language Table environment (Lynch & Sermanet, 2020) for learning geometric rearrangements of blocks on a table. We train an image-goal conditioned VLM policy to predict language instructions and the motor controls from the start and goal images using the PALM-E architecture (Driess et al., 2023) (See data and model details in Appendix D.1). For the baseline, the goal is set to the last frame of the original short-horizon trajectories. During each evaluation run, we set the long-horizon goal by modifying the location of 3-4 blocks, and measure the blocks’ distance to their goal states after executing 5 instructions using the VLM policy. **Generating Hindsight Data with the Simulator.** To use the simulator for long-horizon tasks, we draw inspiration from hindsight relabeling (Rauber et al., 2019). Specifically, we create a total of 10k long-horizon trajectories from the simulator by doing rollouts in the simulator 3-5 times per trajectory, where each rollout corresponds to one scripted language instruction. **Results on Real-Robot Evaluation.** Despite the VLM policy only being trained on simulated data, it is able to produce effective high-level language actions given an initial and goal image from the real Language Table domain where the data for training the simulator was collected. The simulator can simulate video trajectories from the initial real observation, from which robot actions are recovered using an inverse dynamics model and executed on the real robot. Figure 7 shows that the language actions produced by the VLM, the generated videos from the simulator according to the language actions, and the executions on the real robot. We see that the simulated video trajectory is successfully translated to robot actions in the real world. See additional results from the longhorizon VLM policy in Appendix A.2.

**Results on Simulated Evaluation.** In addition to testing the language instructions and simulated video by converting video trajectory into robot actions executed on the real robot, we also conduct simulator based evaluation to compare the reduction in distance to goal (RDG) of the VLM policy using generated long-horizon data to using the original short-horizon data in Table 2. The VLM trained using long-horizon generated data performs 3-4 times better than using the original data in completing long-horizon goal-conditioned tasks.

## 4.2 REAL-WORLD SIMULATOR FOR REINFORCEMENT LEARNING

Reinforcement learning (RL) has achieved superhuman performance on difficult tasks such as playing Go and Atari games (Silver et al.; Mnih et al., 2015), but has limited real world applications due, among other reasons, to the lack of a realistic environment simulator (Dulac-Arnold et al., 2019). We investigate whether the simulator can enable effective training of RL agents by providing the agent with a realistic simulator that can be accessed in parallel.

**Setup.** We finetune the PaLI 3B vision-language model (Chen et al., 2022b) to predict low-level control actions (joint movements in ∆ _x,_ ∆ _y_ ) from an image observation and a task description (e.g., “move the blue cube to the right”) using behavioral cloning (BC) to serve as the low-level control policy and the baseline, which we call the vision-language-action (VLA) policy similar to Brohan et al. (2023). Because UniSim can take low-level control actions as input, we can directly conduct model-based rollouts in the simulator using control actions generated by VLA policy. To acquire reward information, we use the number of steps-to-completion from the training data as a proxy reward to train a model that maps the current observation to learned reward. We then use the

## 4.3 REALISTIC SIMULATOR FOR BROADER VISION-LANGUAGE TASKS

UniSim can generate training data for other machine-learning subproblems. This is especially useful when natural data is rare or difficult to collect (e.g., footage of crimes or accidents). We provide such a proof-of-concept by training vision-language models on purely generated data from UniSim, and observe significant performance benefits in video captioning.

**Setup.** We finetune PaLI-X (Chen et al., 2023), a VLM with 55B parameters pretrained on a broad set of image, video, and language tasks, to caption a set of videos generated by UniSim using texts from the

training split of ActivityNet Captions (Krishna et al., 2017). We measure the CIDEr score of the finetuned model on the test split of ActivityNet Captions as well as other captioning tasks following the same setup as Chen et al. (2023). See finetuning details of PaLI-X in Appendix D.3.

**Results.** We compare PaLI-X finetuned on purely generated videos to pretrained PaLI-X without finetuning and PaLI-X finetuned on original ActivityNet Captions in Table 4. Purely finetuning on generated data drastically improves the captioning performance from no finetuning at all on ActivityNet (15.2 to 46.23), while achieving 84% performance of finetuning on true data. Furthermore, PaLI-X finetuned on generated data transfers better to other captioning tasks such as MSR-VTT (Xu et al., 2016), VATEX (Wang et al., 2019), and SMIT (Monfort et al., 2021) than PaLI-X finetuned on true data, which tends to overfit to ActivityNet. These results suggest that UniSim can serve as an effective data generator for improving broader vision-language models.

## 5 RELATED WORK

**Internet-Scale Generative Models.** Language models trained on internet text succeed at text-based tasks (OpenAI, 2023; Anil et al., 2023) but not physical tasks, which requires perception and control. Internet-scale generative models can synthesize realistic images and videos (Wu et al., 2021; Ho et al., 2022a; Singer et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2023; Blattmann et al., 2023), but have mostly

8

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2024

been applied to generative media (Zhang et al., 2023) as opposed to empowering sophisticated agents capable of multi-turn interactions. Du et al. (2023a) shows video generation can serve as policies, but the major bottleneck for policy learning often lies in limited access to real-world environments (Dulac-Arnold et al., 2019). We focus on this exact bottleneck by learning universal simulators of the real world, enabling realistic and unlimited “environment” access for training sophisticated agents interactively.

**Learning World Models.** Learning an accurate dynamics model in reaction to control inputs has been a long-standing challenge in system identification (Ljung & Glad, 1994), model-based reinforcement learning (Sutton, 1991), and optimal control Astr¨om & Wittenmark[˚] (1973); Bertsekas (1995). Most systems choose to learn one dynamics model per system in the lower dimensional state space as opposed to in the pixel space (Ferns et al., 2004; Achille & Soatto, 2018; Lesort et al., 2018; Castro, 2020), which, despite being a simpler modeling problem, limits knowledge sharing across systems. With large transformer architectures, learning image-based world models has become plausible (Hafner et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2022a; Seo et al., 2022; Micheli et al., 2022; Wu et al., 2022; Hafner et al., 2023), but mostly in games or simulated domains with visually simplistic and abundant data. In generative modeling of videos, previous works have leveraged text prompts (Yu et al., 2023; Zhou et al., 2022), driving motions (Siarohin et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2022), 3D geometries (Weng et al., 2019; Xue et al., 2018), physical simulations (Chuang et al., 2005), frequency information (Li et al., 2023), and user annotations (Hao et al., 2018) to introduce movements into videos. However, they focus on generating domain specific videos (e.g., for self-driving) as opposed to building a universal simulator that can be used to further improve other agents. The amount of control over generated videos in these existing work is also limited, as they do not treat video generation as a dynamics modeling problem like in our work.

## 6 LIMITATIONS AND CONCLUSION

We have shown it is possible to learn a simulator of the real world in response to various action inputs ranging from texts to robot controls. UniSim can simulate visually realistic experiences for interacting with humans and training autonomous agents. We hope UniSim will instigate broad interest in learning and applying real-world simulators to improve machine intelligence. Our simulator has a few limitations that call for future work:

- **Hallucination.** When an action is unrealistic given the scene (e.g., “wash hands” is given to a tabletop robot), we observe hallucinations (e.g., the table turns into a sink or the view turns away from the tabletop robot and a sink shows up). Ideally, we want UniSim to detect actions that are not possible to simulate as opposed to hallucinating unrealistic outcomes.

- **Limited memory.** The simulator conditioned on a few frames of the recent history cannot capture long-term memory (e.g., an apple in a drawer could disappear when the drawer is opened if putting the apple in the drawer is not a part of the history for conditioning). How much history to condition on depends on the application of the (e.g., whether the simulator will be used for policy learning in a near-Markov setting or question answering that requires long-term memory).

- **Limited out-of-domain generalization.** This is especially true for domains that are not represented in the training data. For instance, the simulator is mostly trained on 4 robot morphologies, and its ability to generalize to an unseen robot is limited. Further scaling up training data could help, as the training data is nowhere near all the video data available on the internet.

- **Visual simulation only.** Our simulator is not suitable for environments where actions do not cause visual observation change (e.g., different forces in grasping a static cup). A true universal simulator should capture all aspects of the world beyond visual experience (e.g., sound, sensory, etc).

## B DATASETS

We provide the datasets used to train UniSim below, including dataset name, number of training examples (approximate), and weight in the data mixture. Miscellaneous data are collections of datasets that have not been published. Some of these datasets have been processed into train and validation split, hence the number of training examples may differ from the original data size. When text are available in the original dataset, we use T5 language model embeddings (Raffel et al., 2020) to preprocess the text into continuous representations. When low-level controls are available in the original dataset, we encode them both as text and normalize then discretize them into 4096 bins contatenated with language embeddings (if present). The choice of mixture weights are either 0.1 or 0.05 without careful tuning. How data mixture weights affect simulation performance is an interesting line of future work.

## C ARCHITECTURE AND TRAINING

We the 3D U-Net architecture (C¸ ic¸ek et al., 2016; Ho et al., 2022b) to parametrize UniSim video model. We apply the spatial downsampling pass followed by the spatial upsampling pass with skip connections to the downsampling pass activations with interleaved 3D convolution and attention layers as in the standard 3D U-Net. The video models in UniSim consist of one history conditioned video prediction model as the base and two additional spatial super-resolution models similar to Ho et al. (2022a). The history conditioned base model operates at temporal and spatial resolution [16 _,_ 24 _,_ 40], and the two spatial super-resolution models operate at spatial resolution [24 _,_ 40] _→_ [48 _,_ 80] and [48 _,_ 80] _→_ [192 _,_ 320], respectively. To condition the base video model on the history, we take 4 frames from the previous video segment and concatenate them channelwise to the noise samples inputted to the U-Net. We employ temporal attention for the forward model to allow maximum modeling flexibility but temporal convolution to the super-resolution models for efficiency reasons similar to Ho et al. (2022a). progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Progressive (gemini-3-flash-preview)
progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: This paper presents a highly ambitious and timely contribution to the field of generative modeling and embodied AI by proposing UniSim, a universal simulator for real-world interactions. The quality of the work is underscored by its successful integration of disparate data regimes—ranging from static web-scale images to low-level robotics trajectories—into a unified "action-in-video-out" framework. The clarity of the writing is excellent, particularly in how it articulates the central roadblock of data fragmentation and proposes a sensible orchestration strategy to bridge these gaps. Perhaps its most significant contribution is the demonstration of zero-shot sim-to-real transfer for both high-level vision-language policies and low-level control, suggesting that generative video models are maturing into viable training environments. However, the manuscript's primary challenges lie in the technical specifics of temporal consistency and action unification: while the authors mention "careful orchestration" and autoregressive rollouts, the complexity of aligning heterogeneous action spaces (e.g., text vs. $\Delta x, \Delta y$) without catastrophic forgetting or mode collapse across domains remains a critical point of scrutiny. Overall, this represents a significant step toward general-purpose world models.

comments.title: Non-standard and ambiguous Diffusion Sampling Formulation

comments.explanation: The denoising step in Equation 2 uses a non-standard notation that deviates from established DDPM/DDIM frameworks. Specifically, $\alpha^{(k)}$ is described as a 'linear decay' and $\gamma^{(k)}$ as a 'step-size', but their mathematical relationship to the variance schedule $\beta_k$ or the cumulative noise $\bar{\alpha}$ is never derived or defined. In standard models, coefficients scale the noise estimate and the noisy sample separately; here, the placement of $\alpha^{(k)}$ outside the parentheses suggests it scales the entire update. Without explicit definitions, it is impossible to verify if this update rule preserves the variance schedule correctly or satisfies Langevin dynamics, posing a significant risk to reproducibility for a 5.6B parameter model.

While the paper defines the transition as being conditioned on history $h_{t-1}$ (which usually includes past frames and actions in RL contexts) and current action $a_{t-1}$, the text later states they 'omit previous actions as history.' It is ambiguous whether the model relies purely on visual changes to infer state or if the 'omission' refers only to the action embeddings. This is critical in non-Markovian environments where the previous action $a_{t-2}$ clarifies the current state. If the reward model is trained on real-world data, the claim that RL training is 'purely in simulation' is logically suspect, as the supervision signal originates from the real world. The text fails to clarify if the reward for low-level RL is derived from the generative model itself (e.g., via density estimation) or an external pre-trained model, making it difficult to evaluate the autonomy of the simulator as a standalone training environment.

This shifts the definition of 'action' from a physical transformation to a semantic description, and it is unclear how this contributes to the simulator's ability to predict future states in a way that is consistent with the temporal $p(o_t | h_{t-1}, a_{t-1})$ formulation.

comments.title: Discrepancy in Denoising Equation 2
comments.quote: $o_t^{(k-1)} = \alpha^{(k)} (o_t^{(k)} - \gamma^{(k)} \epsilon_\theta (o_t^{(k)}, k | h_{t-1}, a_{t-1})) + \xi, \xi \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_k^2 I)$

Usually, the predicted noise $\epsilon_\theta$ is used to estimate the 'clear' image $x_0$, and then the next step $x_{t-1}$ is sampled. Here, $\gamma^{(k)}$ presumably represents a step-size related to the score, but $\alpha^{(k)}$ is described as a 'linear decay on the current denoised sample.' In typical diffusion models, $\alpha$ coefficients multiply the original image or the noise, not the entire subtracted result in this specific linear combination. Without explicit definitions of $\gamma^{(k)}$ and $\alpha^{(k)}$ in the appendix or text (they are listed as 'parameters' in the summary but not derived), it is difficult to verify if this update rule preserves the variance schedule correctly or if it is a specific variant (like a simplified Euler step for Score-based SDEs). comments.title: Inconsistency in history conditioning notation

If $a_{t-1}$ is omitted from the history $h_{t-1}$, the notation $p(o_t | h_{t-1}, a_{t-1})$ is technically consistent if $h$ only contains frames. While internally consistent, the claim that they 'omit previous actions' suggests $a_{t-2}, a_{t-3}...$ are not used, which is fine, but the reader should be careful not to confuse the action $a_{t-1}$ (the prompt for the current generation) with the 'action history'. comments.title: Ambiguity in Classifier-Free Guidance formulation

By using the exact same symbol for the resulting guided estimate and the conditional model component, the equation technically reads as $X = (1+\eta)X - \eta Y$, which implies $X=Y$ (the action does nothing) unless $\eta=0$. A careful reader understands the LHS is the 'guided' epsilon used in the sampling update (Eq 2), but using the same subscript and symbol for both the operator and one of its terms is notationally recursive and potentially confusing.

comments.title: Potential OCR/Variable duplication in Denoising Step

Here, the authors state $o_t^{(k-1)}$ is a 'linear decay on the current denoised sample' (the term in parentheses). In standard formulations, the denoised estimate $\hat{o}_0$ is often scaled by a coefficient while the direction of the noise $\epsilon_\theta$ is scaled by another. By placing both $o_t^{(k)}$ and $\epsilon_\theta$ inside the parentheses multiplied by $\alpha^{(k)}$, the authors imply that $\alpha^{(k)}$ acts on the 'clean' estimate. While this can be a valid reparameterization, the text says $\gamma^{(k)}$ is the 'denoising step size' and $\alpha^{(k)}$ is a 'linear decay'. If $\alpha^{(k)} < 1$ (as 'decay' suggests), this update step might not properly preserve the variance of the marginal distribution unless $\xi$ is specifically tuned to compensate for the shrinkage of both the signal and the predicted noise. Without the explicit definitions of $\alpha$ and $\gamma$ in terms of $\beta$, a reader cannot verify if this satisfies the conditions for valid Langevin dynamics or DDIM-style sampling.

If $h_{t-1}$ is empty or a padding value, the model might learn to ignore the history when the action is a descriptive caption, or conversely, it might fail to learn dynamics because there is no change ($o_{t-1} \to o_t$) to model. Since the model relies on the delta between frames to understand the effect of an action, it is unclear how training on static (image, caption) pairs—where no 'previous' state exists—actually informs the 'Universal Simulator's' ability to predict transitions, rather than just performing text-to-image generation. progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Progressive (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a compelling and ambitious vision for learning an interactive real-world simulator—UniSim—by unifying diverse datasets under a generative modeling framework. The writing is generally clear and well-structured, with a strong motivation that situates the work within broader goals in embodied AI, sim-to-real transfer, and generative modeling. The core idea—leveraging complementary strengths across disparate data sources (e.g., static visual richness from internet-scale image-text pairs, motion dynamics from robotics and navigation data) to train a unified action-to-video model—is conceptually novel and timely, particularly as the field moves toward more interactive and temporally coherent simulation environments. However, the paper raises several significant concerns that affect its overall clarity and scientific rigor. Most notably, the methodology section lacks sufficient detail: key aspects such as how disparate action spaces are unified (e.g., language instructions vs. low-level robot torques), how temporal misalignments between datasets are resolved, and how the diffusion model architecture handles variable-length video generation are only vaguely described. The claim of zero-shot sim-to-real transfer for both high-level vision-language policies and low-level RL controllers is strong, yet the evaluation of this claim appears to be absent in the provided text, raising questions about empirical validation. Furthermore, while the integration of multi-domain datasets is presented as a strength, the paper does not thoroughly address potential distributional mismatches or domain gaps that could degrade simulation consistency or roll-out fidelity. The claim of enabling "long-horizon" consistent simulation via autoregressive prediction needs more concrete analysis—such as quantitative metrics on temporal coherence or error accumulation—beyond qualitative demos. In summary, the paper proposes an exciting and potentially impactful direction, but its current form prioritizes breadth and vision over technical depth and evaluation, leaving critical questions about model design, training stability, and real-world transfer performance unanswered.

comments.title: Circular definition and notational error in classifier-free guidance equation
comments.explanation: Equation (1) contains a critical notational flaw: the same symbol ϵθ appears on both sides with identical arguments, implying a self-referential identity that is mathematically invalid unless η=0. The intended form is clearly classifier-free guidance: ϵ_guided = (1+η)ϵθ(o,k|c) − ηϵθ(o,k|∅), where the two evaluations of ϵθ use different conditioning (with/without action). The current presentation fails to distinguish the output from the model's internal evaluations, risking misinterpretation and incorrect implementation. Additional OCR issues (e.g., 'ot' vs 'o_t^[k]') compound the confusion, but the core issue is the reuse of ϵθ in a circular manner. Despite the surrounding text and standard knowledge of classifier-free guidance, this constitutes a technical error in the formalism that undermines reproducibility and clarity.

comments.title: Inconsistent and misleading use of history variable $h_{t-1}$ in observation prediction model
comments.quote: At a specific interactive step _t_ , an agent, having observed a set of history frames _ht−_ 1 _∈ O_ , decides on some temporally extended action _at−_ 1 _∈ A_ ... we found conditioning on a finite set of frames (e.g., frames from the most recent interaction, _ot−_ 1) greatly simplifies the modeling problem.
comments.explanation: The paper introduces $h_{t-1}$ as a general history of observations, suggesting cumulative memory as standard in POMDPs or RL. However, it immediately reveals that in practice, $h_{t-1}$ is equivalent to only the most recent observation $o_{t-1}$ or a small window of recent frames. This creates a notational discrepancy: the formalism implies long-term memory, but the implementation uses only short-term visual input. While the authors justify this simplification for tractability, they do not redefine $h_{t-1}$ to reflect this restriction. This misleads readers into assuming the model maintains explicit historical state, when in fact consistency arises from autoregressive video generation and frame-to-frame conditioning. The ambiguity is further reinforced in long-horizon claims, where 'previous observations' could imply persistent memory. To resolve this, $h_{t-1}$ should be explicitly defined as a fixed-length window (e.g., last $n$ frames of $o_{t-1}$) and not be used to denote general history.

comments.title: Underspecified autoregressive rollout procedure for long-horizon simulation
comments.quote: To simulate long interactions, we can sample from the observation prediction model _p_ ( _ot|ht−_ 1 _, at−_ 1) autoregressively conditioned on the previously sampled observations.
comments.explanation: The autoregressive rollout mechanism is central to UniSim's long-horizon simulation claims, yet critical details are missing. The paper does not specify: (1) how many frames are predicted per step (fixed or variable?), (2) how frames are selected and concatenated during rollout (e.g., last $n$ frames), (3) how the 'initial noise sample' for the next segment is concatenated with the previous frame (spatially? temporally?), (4) how conditioning frames are replicated across future indices (tiled identically?), (5) how the rollout is initialized when insufficient history exists (padding?), and (6) how the model maintains temporal coherence across segment boundaries. While Figure 2 and Table 1 hint at some design choices, the absence of a clear procedure in the method section undermines reproducibility. This is especially problematic given the ablation of frame count (Table 1) and claims of multi-step consistency (Figure 4). The reliance on appendix references is insufficient for a core methodological component.

comments.title: Missing training objective for diffusion model
comments.quote: With this parametrization, we train _ϵθ_ by minimizing\n\nwhere _ϵ ∼N_ (0 _, I_ ), and _β_[(] _[k]_[)] _∈_ R are a set of _K_ different noise levels for each _k ∈_ [1 _, K_ ].
comments.explanation: The paper states that ϵθ is trained by minimizing a loss but omits the actual objective—likely due to formatting or OCR error. While the standard denoising objective (e.g., \mathbb{E}[||\epsilon - ϵθ(\cdot)||^2]) is expected, its absence is a critical omission, as the training objective defines the learning process. This gap is exacerbated by the use of classifier-free guidance and a potentially custom noise schedule (β_k), which may influence weighting. Even a placeholder referencing the appendix (e.g., 'see Appendix C') is missing. For a diffusion-based method, this renders the method irreproducible without auxiliary information. The surrounding context does not resolve the omission, as no equivalent formulation is provided in adjacent text.

comments.title: Ambiguous history conditioning implementation in video generation architecture
comments.quote: To condition the base video model on the history, we take 4 frames from the previous video segment and concatenate them channelwise to the noise samples inputted to the U-Net.
comments.explanation: The method describes conditioning via channelwise concatenation of previous frames to 'noise samples', but this raises dimensional and semantic inconsistencies: noise samples are latents in diffusion steps, while past frames are pixel-space observations. It is more likely the intention was to concatenate to the noised input $o_t^{[k]}$. Even under this interpretation, directly concatenating raw frames without learned encoding (e.g., via T5 or a CNN) into the U-Net raises concerns about effective feature extraction. Table 6 lists a 64-token conditioning length and 4096-dimensional embedding, suggesting token-based fusion, which contradicts the described pixel-level concatenation. This discrepancy between textual description and architectural hyperparameters (Table 6) creates confusion about whether conditioning occurs in pixel or embedding space. Given the centrality of history conditioning to long-term coherence, this underspecification hinders understanding and replication.

comments.title: Unspecified or inconsistently defined RDG evaluation metric
comments.quote:
We define the reduction in distance to goal (RDG) metric as

where _sT_ represents the underlying block locations after executing the policy, _s_ 0 and _s_ goal represents the initial and goal block locations.
comments.explanation: The RDG metric is referenced multiple times and used in both Table 2 and the text but lacks a formal mathematical definition. The prose describes the variables involved but omits the formula (e.g., RDG = ||s_0-s_goal|| - ||s_T-s_goal||). This omission prevents replication and interpretation of reported values. Furthermore, Table 2 distinguishes 'RDG(moved)' and 'RDG(all)', implying different state subsets, but the original definition treats RDG as a scalar. The paper does not clarify whether aggregation is by sum, mean, or max, nor the distance metric (L1, L2). Similarly, in the context of VLM evaluation, uncertainty about data normalization or scaling remains. While Appendix D.1 may resolve this, the absence in the main results section compromises transparency for a key metric supporting the paper's claims.

comments.title: Ambiguity in domain identifier usage and generalization implications
comments.quote: While such domain identifier improves in-distribution generation quality, we found domain-specific identifiers to hurt generalization to other domains, and should only be applied with the test domain is in distribution of the training domain.
comments.explanation: The use of dataset prefixes (e.g., 'Habitat:') to improve low-data generation is a key technique, but its operational details are underspecified. It is unclear whether identifiers are used during training, at inference only, or both. Without knowing this, it's difficult to distinguish between learned domain adaptation and test-time prompting. The warning about hurting generalization lacks specificity: what constitutes 'out-of-distribution'? Is it a new dataset, task, or scene type? The lack of clarity on when and how identifiers are applied limits reproducibility, especially for practitioners trying to adapt the method to other domains. Given that this mechanism is central to handling data imbalance, precise description of training and inference protocols is essential.

comments.title: Missing clarification on zero-shot transfer claim for real robot deployment
comments.quote: Both the high-level vision-language policy and the low-level control policy, while trained purely in simulation, can generalize to real robot settings.
comments.explanation: The claim of 'zero-shot transfer' is central to the paper's contribution. However, the use of an inverse dynamics model to map simulated trajectories to real robot actions suggests a potential reliance on real-world calibration data. If this model is trained on real robot executions (even minimally), the deployment is not strictly zero-shot, as it involves real data. The paper does not clarify whether the inverse model requires real-world data or is purely simulated. This ambiguity risks overstating the transfer capabilities and could mislead readers into believing no real-world interaction was needed. The distinction is crucial for assessing the practical applicability and true generalization of the approach.

comments.title: Unjustified architectural choice of temporal convolutions in super-resolution models
comments.quote: We employ temporal attention for the forward model ... but temporal convolution to the super-resolution models for efficiency reasons similar to Ho et al. (2022a).
comments.explanation: The paper cites efficiency as the motivation for using temporal convolutions instead of attention in super-resolution stages, referencing Ho et al. (2022a). However, that work actually uses spatio-temporal attention, not temporal convolutions, undermining the justification. Given UniSim's focus on long-horizon temporal consistency, replacing attention with convolutions in later stages may harm the model's ability to maintain coherent dynamics at higher spatial resolutions. The paper provides no ablation or analysis of this design choice, nor explains why convolutions suffice for spatial enhancement without affecting temporal fidelity. This trade-off, being central to video quality and simulation reliability, requires stronger empirical or theoretical justification.

comments.title: Insufficient description of stochastic diversity and controllable generation mechanism
comments.quote: On the left, we use text to specify the object being revealed by suffixing “uncovering” with the object name. On the right, we only specify “put cup” or “put pen”, and cups and pens of different colors are sampled as a result of the stochastic sampling process during video generation.
comments.explanation: The paper demonstrates controlled stochasticity as a strength, where specific object names lead to deterministic outcomes and generic actions allow variation. However, it does not specify how the model distinguishes between controllable and free variables. Are there architectural mechanisms (e.g., selective classifier-free guidance)? Training strategies? Latent space priors? Without this, it is unclear whether stochastic diversity emerges from diffusion noise alone or from intentional design. This is particularly important for the claim that 'a policy can learn to control only the controllable part.' The lack of methodological detail about how partial action specifications are handled limits reproducibility and understanding of the control-generation interface.

progressive_original__qwen3-235b-a22b-25.label: Progressive Original (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive_original__qwen3-235b-a22b-25.overall_feedback: The paper presents a compelling and ambitious vision for learning an interactive real-world simulator—UniSim—by unifying diverse datasets under a generative modeling framework. The writing is generally clear and well-structured, with a strong motivation that situates the work within broader goals in embodied AI, sim-to-real transfer, and generative modeling. The core idea—leveraging complementary strengths across disparate data sources (e.g., static visual richness from internet-scale image-text pairs, motion dynamics from robotics and navigation data) to train a unified action-to-video model—is conceptually novel and timely, particularly as the field moves toward more interactive and temporally coherent simulation environments. However, the paper raises several significant concerns that affect its overall clarity and scientific rigor. Most notably, the methodology section lacks sufficient detail: key aspects such as how disparate action spaces are unified (e.g., language instructions vs. low-level robot torques), how temporal misalignments between datasets are resolved, and how the diffusion model architecture handles variable-length video generation are only vaguely described. The claim of zero-shot sim-to-real transfer for both high-level vision-language policies and low-level RL controllers is strong, yet the evaluation of this claim appears to be absent in the provided text, raising questions about empirical validation. Furthermore, while the integration of multi-domain datasets is presented as a strength, the paper does not thoroughly address potential distributional mismatches or domain gaps that could degrade simulation consistency or roll-out fidelity. The claim of enabling "long-horizon" consistent simulation via autoregressive prediction needs more concrete analysis—such as quantitative metrics on temporal coherence or error accumulation—beyond qualitative demos. In summary, the paper proposes an exciting and potentially impactful direction, but its current form prioritizes breadth and vision over technical depth and evaluation, leaving critical questions about model design, training stability, and real-world transfer performance unanswered.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of history variable in observation prediction model
comments.quote: At a specific interactive step _t_ , an agent, having observed a set of history frames _ht−_ 1 _∈ O_ , decides on some temporally extended action _at−_ 1 _∈ A_ , which can be resolved into a sequence of low-level robot commands to be executed in the real world. ... The goal of a simulator is to predict _ot_ from _ht−_ 1 and _at−_ 1. We can formulate this prediction problem as learning an _observation prediction_ model _p_ ( _ot|ht−_ 1 _, at−_ 1).
comments.explanation: The paper introduces _ht−1_ as the full history of observations up to step _t−1_, but then immediately simplifies the conditioning to only recent observations (e.g., _ot−1_) for tractability. This shift is justified in the text: 'we found conditioning on a finite set of frames (e.g., frames from the most recent interaction, _ot−1_) greatly simplifies the modeling problem.' Thus, the intended meaning is that _ht−1_ is not the complete history but a finite window, likely equivalent to _ot−1_ in practice. However, the notation _ht−1_ suggests a cumulative history (standard in POMDPs and RL), while the actual model uses only the most recent observation(s). This creates a notational inconsistency between the formal definition and implementation. Although the authors clarify their simplification, they do not redefine _ht−1_ to match this usage. A better approach would be to redefine _ht−1_ as the most recent observation window or use distinct notation (e.g., _o_hist_) to avoid confusion. As written, a careful reader might initially believe the model conditions on full history, which is not the case.

comments.title: Mismatch between diffusion model output and observation space
comments.quote: We parametrize _p_ ( _ot|ht−_ 1 _, at− 1_) using diffusion models ... Specifically, the reverse process learns a denoising model _ϵθ_ ( _o_[(] _t[k]_[)] _, k|ht−_ 1 _, at−_ 1) that, conditioned on the history, generates the next observation from initial noise samples using _K_ denoising steps.
comments.explanation: The denoising model _ϵθ_ is described as generating 'the next observation' from noise. However, in standard diffusion models, _ϵθ_ predicts the noise added to the data, not the observation itself. The actual generation is done via the reverse process in Equation (2), where the prediction of _ϵθ_ is used to iteratively refine the sample. The phrasing 'generates the next observation' is misleading—it conflates the role of the denoising network with the full sampling process. While this is a common informal shorthand in the literature, it risks misleading readers unfamiliar with diffusion models into thinking _ϵθ_ is a direct generator. Given the technical nature of the section and the presence of formal equations, precision is expected. The subsequent Equation (2) correctly describes the reverse process, which resolves much of the confusion, but the prose still overstates the role of _ϵθ_. A minor rewording (e.g., 'used to generate') would fix this.

comments.title: Ambiguity in classifier-free guidance formulation
comments.quote:
The final _T_ ( _ot|ht−_ 1 _, at−_ 1) is parametrized by the variance schedule:

_ϵθ_ ( _o_[(] _t[k]_[)] _, k|ht−_ 1 _, at−_ 1) = (1 + _η_ ) _ϵθ_ ( _o_[(] _t[k]_[)] _, k|ht−_ 1 _, at−_ 1) _− ηϵθ_ ( _ot, k|ht−_ 1) _,_ (1)
comments.explanation:
Equation (1) appears to redefine _ϵθ_ on the left-hand side using _ϵθ_ on the right-hand side — a circular definition. This suggests a notation error. The intended form is almost certainly:

_ϵ_\_\text{guided} = (1 + _η_) _ϵθ_(_o_,k|_h_,_a_) − _η_ _ϵθ_(_o_,k|_h_,∅_),

where the second term conditions on no action (null condition). The current notation uses _ϵθ_ for both the guided and unguided versions, which is incorrect. Moreover, the second term uses _ot_ instead of _o_[(] _t[k]_[)] , which breaks dimensional consistency — _ot_ is the full observation, while _o_[(] _t[k]_[)] is the noisy version at step _k_. This is likely an OCR or typesetting issue: 'ot' should be 'o_t^[k]'. Even accounting for that, the reuse of _ϵθ_ on both sides is a critical flaw. Given that classifier-free guidance is a standard technique (Ho & Salimans, 2022), the correct form is well-known, so experts may mentally correct it. But for reproducibility, the equation must distinguish the guided output from the model predictions. This constitutes a technical error in the formula as presented.

comments.title: Underspecified autoregressive rollout procedure
comments.quote: To simulate long interactions, we can sample from the observation prediction model _p_ ( _ot|ht−_ 1 _, at−_ 1) autoregressively conditioned on the previously sampled observations.
comments.explanation: While the idea of autoregressive rollout is clear, the exact procedure for handling the transition between video segments is underspecified. The text mentions in Figure 2 caption: 'Each dotted arrow indicates concatenating the initial noise sample for the next video segment with the previous frame.' However, the method section does not explain how the 'previous frame' is selected (last frame of _ot_?), how noise is concatenated (channel-wise? temporal padding?), or how the model handles variable-length outputs. This is especially important because the simulator is claimed to produce 'consistent and long-horizon videos'. The architecture section says: 'we replicate the conditioning frames at all future frame indices, and concatenate... with the noise sample', but doesn't specify how many frames are used as history or how they are selected during rollout. Table 1 shows ablations over 1, 4 distant, and 4 recent frames, but the default setting is not explicitly stated. For a method central to long-horizon simulation, these details are essential for reproducibility. While some might be in Appendix C, their absence here makes the method underspecified.

comments.title: Inconsistent subscript formatting in denoising process equation
comments.explanation:
The formatting of subscripts and time indices in Equation (2) is garbled due to OCR. The left-hand side appears to be _o_t^[k−1]_, but is rendered as _o_[(] _t[k][−]_[1)]. Similarly, _σk_ should be _σ_k_, and _N_(0, σ_k² I) has mangled brackets. The structure matches standard DDPM reverse process, so the intended meaning is recoverable. For example, the denoising step should be:

o_t^{k-1} = α_k (o_t^k − γ_k ϵ_θ(o_t^k, k | h, a)) + ξ,   ξ ~ N(0, σ_k² I)

However, the current rendering could confuse a reader trying to implement it, especially the 'σk_[2]' and '[I]' notation. Since OCR issues are acknowledged in the instructions, and the intended symbols can be inferred from context and standard diffusion formulations, this is not flagged as a true error. But it illustrates the risk of poor OCR in critical equations.

comments.title: Incorrect Denoising Formula in Equation (1)
comments.explanation:
At first glance, the left-hand side and the first term on the right-hand side of Equation (1) appear identical: both are _ϵθ_(_o_[t[k]], k|ht−1, at−1). This would imply that the equation reduces to _ϵθ_ = (1+η)_ϵθ_ − η_ϵθ_(unconditional), suggesting (1+η)_ϵθ_(conditioned) − _ϵθ_(conditioned) = η_ϵθ_(unconditional), which only makes sense if _ϵθ_ on the left is the *output*, and the one on the right is the *input* — but that’s not standard notation. In classifier-free guidance, the correct form is: 

\hat{\epsilon}_\theta = (1+\eta)\epsilon_\theta(x, c) - \eta\epsilon_\theta(x, \emptyset),

where the same function \epsilon_\theta is evaluated under two different conditioning inputs (with and without the class label). The way it's written here, with the full _ϵθ_ appearing on both sides, suggests a recursive definition or self-reference that doesn't make sense. The repeated use of the *same* symbol _ϵθ_(_o_[t[k]], k|ht−1, at−1) on both sides — before and after the equality — is not merely notationally sloppy; it is mathematically incorrect unless explicitly clarified as shorthand.

I checked the original classifier-free guidance paper (Ho & Salimans, 2022), and the standard formulation uses two evaluations: one conditioned and one unconditionally. The authors attempt to match this, but the notation fails because they reuse _ϵθ_ on the left and inside the right-hand side with identical arguments. The intended meaning is clear: this is classifier-free guidance combining the conditional and unconditional denoiser. However, the way it is currently written makes it seem as if _ϵθ_ is defined in terms of itself.

A correct version would distinguish the final output from the internal evaluations, e.g.,

\epsilon_{\text{final}} = (1+\eta)\epsilon_\theta(o_{t[k]}, k \mid h_{t−1}, a_{t−1}) - \eta \epsilon_\theta(o_{t[k]}, k \mid h_{t−1}),

or simply define the final denoising target using two separate terms. As written, this risks misleading readers about the actual computation graph or causing implementation errors.

comments.title: Ambiguous Noise Initialisation and Conditioning in Autoregressive Sampling
comments.quote: Each dotted arrow indicates concatenating the initial noise sample for the next video segment with the previous frame.
comments.explanation:
The caption for Figure 2 states that the initial noise sample for the next video segment is concatenated with the previous frame (presumably the last observed frame from the prior rollout). Later, in the text under 'Parametrizing and Training the Simulator', it says: “concatenate previous video frames with initial noise samples _o_[(] _t[K]_[)] _∼N_ (0 _, I_ ) channelwise to serve as conditional inputs”.

At first, this seems coherent: the model conditions on past frames by concatenating them to the noisy future frames. But a deeper issue arises during autoregressive rollout: when generating the next segment _ot_, we are to concatenate *its* initial noise _o_[t[K]] ∼ N(0,I) with the *previous* observation (e.g., _ot−1_). But _o_[t[K]] is random and uncorrelated with _ot−1_. Concatenating a fresh noise sample channelwise to a real video frame could create unnatural edge effects or misalignments unless properly handled (e.g., via learned adapters or spatial masking).

More critically, the text says “we replicate the conditioning frames at all future frame indices”, implying that a single frame (or few frames) from _ot−1_ are tiled across all future frame positions in _ot_. But this raises ambiguity: is the same frame repeated across all _T_ predicted frames? If so, how does the model know the temporal structure? The architecture uses a video U-Net with temporal attention, so it may learn to attend appropriately. Still, the interaction between the replicated conditioning frames and the randomly initialized future frames concatenated channelwise is underspecified.

The concern is whether the conditioning mechanism actually preserves sufficient temporal coherence across rollouts. The authors show visually consistent long-horizon simulations, so it likely works in practice. However, the method description does not clarify *how* the conditioning avoids degeneracy at segment boundaries, nor how the random noise interacts with deterministic past frames. This leaves a reproduction gap: a reader might not know whether to tile the last frame across all time steps of the noise volume, or how spatial alignment is managed. Given the importance of autoregressive consistency in UniSim’s claims, this level of underspecification is problematic.

comments.title: Mismatch Between History Definition (ht−1) and Practical Implementation (ot−1)
comments.quote: At a specific interactive step _t_ , an agent, having observed a set of history frames _ht−_ 1 _∈ O_ , decides on some temporally extended action _at−_ 1 _∈ A_ ... we found conditioning on a finite set of frames (e.g., frames from the most recent interaction, _ot−_ 1) greatly simplifies the modeling problem.
comments.explanation:
The formal definition introduces a history _ht−1_ ∈ O as the conditioning input, implying a potentially arbitrary sequence of past observations. However, the text immediately says they only condition on _ot−1_, the most recent observation. This creates a notational mismatch: _ht−1_ is defined as a 'set of history frames', but then equated in practice to just _ot−1_, a single video clip.

One might argue that _ht−1_ could simply be defined as the immediately prior observation _ot−1_, and that’s what they’re using. However, the subscript notation suggests a cumulative history (like h_t = (o_0, a_0, ..., o_{t−1})), especially since the paper contrasts this with conditioning on the full past. Yet in the actual model, no recurrence or memory is used — only the last video segment.

This isn't a major logical flaw, since the authors are upfront about simplifying the conditioning. The issue is that the prose presents _ht−1_ as a general history, but the method uses only _ot−1_. A careful reader might expect some recurrent state or memory module to maintain longer-term context, especially for tasks like long-horizon rearrangement. Instead, consistency is achieved through autoregressive generation with overlapping or preserved states in the video content itself.

The claim of long-horizon consistency (e.g., object persistence in Figure 4) then relies not on explicit memory in _ht−1_, but on the generative model’s ability to preserve scene state in the video output, which it then conditions on in the next step. This is valid, but the formalism misleadingly suggests a separation between memory (h) and current observation (o), which isn’t reflected in implementation.

Renaming _ht−1_ to _o_{t−1}_ throughout would eliminate this ambiguity and better reflect the actual model design.

comments.title: Underspecified Training Objective for Diffusion Model
comments.quote: With this parametrization, we train _ϵθ_ by minimizing\n\nwhere _ϵ ∼N_ (0 _, I_ ), and _β_[(] _[k]_[)] _∈_ R are a set of _K_ different noise levels for each _k ∈_ [1 _, K_ ].
comments.explanation:
The text states they train _ϵθ_ by minimizing a loss, but the actual loss function is missing — the sentence ends with 'by minimizing' and then jumps to the definition of _β_[k]. This is clearly a formatting or OCR error, as the loss should be the usual denoising objective:

\mathbb{E}_{o,ϵ,k}\left[ \| \epsilon - \epsilon_\theta(o_{t[k]}, k \mid h_{t−1}, a_{t−1}) \|^2 \right].

While this is standard in diffusion literature, and experts would assume it, the paper does not state it — not even in an abbreviated form. In a standalone section describing the parametrization and training, omitting the optimization objective renders the method irreproducible.

I checked the full context: the paper mentions minimizing a loss but provides no formula. Appendix C is referenced for details, but within the presented text, this is a critical omission. Even if the loss is conventional, its omission here — especially after introducing a modified classifier-free guidance scheme — leaves a gap. Does the loss include a weighting by _β_[k]? Is it reweighted? The _β_[k] appear in the noise schedule, so likely the loss uses standard noise-level weighting, but this isn't confirmed.

Given that this is a core technical component of UniSim, the absence of the training objective (even a placeholder like '[detailed in Appendix C]') constitutes an underspecification.

comments.title: Inconsistent Use of ot−1 vs. ot in Figure 2 Caption
comments.quote: UniSim is a video diffusion model trained to predict the next (variable length) set of observation frames ( _ot_ ) given observations from the past (e.g., _ot−_ 1) and action input _at−_ 1.
comments.explanation:
The caption says the model predicts _ot_ given _ot−1_ and _at−1_, which aligns with standard autoregressive notation. However, in the formal definition a few lines later, the model is defined as _p_( _ot_ | _ht−1_, _at−1_), and _ht−1_ is said to contain _ot−1_, suggesting _ot−1_ is part of the history — not the direct input.

This is minor and likely just a simplification in the caption: using _ot−1_ as a shorthand for 'recent history'. The figure caption is informal, and the full description clarifies that the conditionings are on history frames, which include _ot−1_.

No real issue remains: the intent is clear, and the formalism corrects the simplification. This is excusable in an explanatory caption.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of history variable in long-horizon simulation description
comments.quote: We condition the simulation of each interaction on previous observations and new language action as described in Section 2.2.
comments.explanation: At first glance, this statement appears consistent with the model definition in Section 2.2, where the observation prediction model conditions on $ h_{t-1} $, interpreted as recent observation frames such as $ o_{t-1} $. The authors explicitly state their assumption that conditioning on a finite set of recent frames suffices, which justifies using only the immediate past. However, in the context of *long-horizon* simulation (e.g., Figure 4 showing 8 sequential interactions), the phrase 'previous observations' could reasonably be interpreted by a careful reader as implying persistent state or memory across many steps. The concern is whether the model truly maintains consistency over many rollouts through only short-term history (e.g., last frame or four frames), or if there's an implicit assumption of history accumulation not specified in the formalism. While the autoregressive rollout mechanism described in Section 2.2 supports this behavior in principle (each output becomes part of the next input), the text does not clarify *how much* history is carried forward—whether it's a fixed window or grows over time. This becomes relevant when evaluating claims of temporal consistency (e.g., preserving object states over 8 steps), as such consistency may require internal memory beyond what's formalized. The ablation in Table 1 shows that 4 recent frames perform best, suggesting a fixed context window is used. This likely resolves the issue—consistency emerges from iterative conditioning on recent video segments rather than recurrent state. Still, the prose could mislead readers into thinking the full interaction trajectory is retained. A minor clarification would help: explicitly stating that a fixed number of most recent frames (e.g., last 4) are used as $ h_{t-1} $ at each step.

comments.title: Ambiguity in conditioning strategy for stochastic diversity (Figure 5)
comments.quote: On the left, we use text to specify the object being revealed by suffixing “uncovering” with the object name. On the right, we only specify “put cup” or “put pen”, and cups and pens of different colors are sampled as a result of the stochastic sampling process during video generation.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly highlight the model’s ability to generate diverse and stochastic outcomes, which is a strength of diffusion-based generation. However, the description raises a subtle ambiguity about action specification and controllability. On the left, they claim control by explicitly naming the uncovered object (e.g., 'uncover spider'), implying that such outcomes are action-conditioned and thus reproducible. On the right, they allow stochastic variation in color and location. The issue lies in distinguishing between *controlled randomness* and *uncontrolled generation*. If the action is merely 'put cup', then the model must infer default attributes (color, position), which is reasonable. But without clarification, a reader might wonder whether the stochasticity is due to diffusion noise alone or reflects underspecified conditioning. More critically, this touches on the claim that 'a policy can learn to only control the controllable part'—a valuable insight. Yet, the method section doesn't specify how the action representation handles partial specifications: does the model learn to treat certain aspects as free variables? Is there an implicit prior over object attributes? While diffusion models naturally support diverse sampling from the same conditioning, the text does not describe whether specific architectural or training choices (e.g., classifier-free guidance applied selectively) help modulate controllability. The discussion is plausible and aligns with known properties of diffusion models, but the lack of specification makes it hard to assess reproducibility. This is not a flaw in logic, but an underspecification of the mechanism enabling controlled stochasticity.

comments.title: Missing definition of RDG metric despite its introduction
comments.quote:
We define the reduction in distance to goal (RDG) metric as

where _sT_ represents the underlying block locations after executing the policy, _s_ 0 and _s_ goal represents the initial and goal block locations.
comments.explanation: The passage introduces a new evaluation metric, RDG, and claims it is defined, but then fails to provide the actual formula. The sentence structure suggests the equation should follow immediately, but it is missing—likely due to a formatting or extraction error in the OCR process. This is not a conceptual flaw per se, but it creates a significant gap in methodological transparency. Although the variables are described (sT, s0, s_goal), the actual computation (e.g., RDG = ||s0 - s_goal|| - ||sT - s_goal|| or normalized variants) is absent. In reinforcement learning and robotics, distance-based metrics can vary (L1, L2, mean, max), so omitting the form prevents replication. However, since RDG is introduced in the context of a baseline comparison and not used quantitatively in this passage, and given the note that the text may be cut off ('We then use the'), this may simply be an artifact of the passage boundary. A full paper would presumably include the equation shortly after. Still, if this were a standalone section, the absence would constitute an underspecification. Given the forward reference and the likelihood of a truncation, this is more of an OCR/text boundary issue than a true methodological omission. But as presented, it remains problematic for reproducibility.

comments.title: Potential confusion in classifier-free guidance equation reference
comments.quote:
where _η_ controls action conditioning strength. With this parametrization, we train _ϵθ_ by minimizing

where _ϵ ∼N_ (0 _, I_ ), and _β_[(] _[k]_[)] _∈_ R are a set of _K_ different noise levels for each _k ∈_ [1 _, K_ ].
comments.explanation:
Shortly after presenting Equation (1) for classifier-free guidance, the text refers to training by minimizing a loss involving ϵ and β[k], but the actual loss function is missing (likely due to OCR/formatting gaps). However, the main concern is not the missing loss, but the notation in Equation (1) itself, which appears in two places: once in the PAPER SUMMARY and again in Section 2.2. The version shown in the summary is:

$$ 
\epsilon_\theta(o_t^{[k]}, k | h_{t-1}, a_{t-1}) = (1 + \eta) \epsilon_\theta(o_t^{[k]}, k | h_{t-1}, a_{t-1}) - \eta \epsilon_\theta(o_t^{[k]}, k | h_{t-1})
$$

This is *circular*—the left-hand side and the first term on the right are identical. This suggests a notation error. The standard form of classifier-free guidance is:

$$
\epsilon_\theta^{\text{guided}} = (1+\eta)\epsilon_\theta(o, k | a) - \eta \epsilon_\theta(o, k | \emptyset)
$$

That is, it combines the conditional and unconditional predictions. The authors almost certainly mean:

$$
\epsilon_\theta^{\text{guided}} = (1+\eta) \epsilon_\theta(o_t^{[k]}, k | h_{t-1}, a_{t-1}) - \eta \epsilon_\theta(o_t^{[k]}, k | h_{t-1})
$$

But in the equation as written, both sides use the same symbol $ \epsilon_\theta $, making it appear as if the model output equals a linear combination of itself and another term, which is mathematically invalid unless η=0. This is a *mathematical error* in the formal presentation. The surrounding text (e.g., 'leveraging classifier-free guidance') and the correct use of η suggest the intent is standard. Moreover, the equation in Section 2.2 is likely identical but truncated in the OCR. Still, even assuming the correct intended meaning, the current rendering misrepresents the method. This would mislead a reader attempting to implement the model. It is not resolved by context alone unless one already knows the standard form. Therefore, this qualifies as a real issue: a flawed equation that contradicts conventional notation and mathematical consistency, even if the underlying idea is sound.

comments.title: Typo in Classifier-Free Guidance Equation Causes Apparent Self-Reference
comments.quote: $ \epsilon_\theta(o_t^{[k]}, k | h_{t-1}, a_{t-1}) = (1 + \eta) \epsilon_\theta(o_t^{[k]}, k | h_{t-1}, a_{t-1}) - \eta \epsilon_\theta(o_t^{[k]}, k | h_{t-1}) $ (Equation 1, classifier-free guidance)
comments.explanation:
At first glance, this equation appears nonsensical: the left-hand side is identical to one of the terms on the right. This would imply $ \epsilon_\theta(\cdot) = (1 + \eta) \epsilon_\theta(\cdot) - \eta \epsilon_\theta(\cdot|\text{no action}) $, which simplifies to $ \epsilon_\theta = \epsilon_\theta + \eta(\epsilon_\theta - \epsilon_\theta(\cdot|\text{no action})) $, leading to $ \eta(\epsilon_\theta - \epsilon_\theta(\cdot|\text{no action})) = 0 $ — only true if $ \eta=0 $ or perfect alignment between conditional and unconditional models, which is not the case. However, knowing classifier-free guidance convention (Ho & Salimans, 2022), the intended form should be:

$$
\epsilon_\theta^{\text{guided}} = (1 + \eta) \epsilon_\theta(o_t^{[k]}, k | h_{t-1}, a_{t-1}) - \eta \epsilon_\theta(o_t^{[k]}, k | h_{t-1})
$$

That is, the left-hand side should be a *guided* (renamed or primed) version of the denoising prediction. The current form incorrectly reuses the same symbol $ \epsilon_\theta $ on both sides. This is not a merely typographic issue because it suggests a mathematical identity that does not hold; it would mislead a reader trying to implement the algorithm. Given that the rest of the paper depends on this conditioning mechanism, and that the error persists in both the summary and the main text, it is likely an OCR or authoring error where the left-hand side was meant to be distinct (e.g., $ \epsilon_\theta^{\text{cf}} $). Even under generous interpretation, the equation as written implies the output equals a weighted combination that includes itself — an invalid recurrence. The paper correctly describes 'classifier-free guidance' in text, so the intent is clear, but the equation is technically incorrect.
